chapters.title: Pushing yourself is not the only solution to reaching your goals
chapters.text:
Many people celebrate New Year's Eve with a glass of champagne in one hand and a list of resolutions in the other. For most, the beginning of a new year signifies the potential for a transformation of their body or financial situation. Nevertheless, according to the studies, only 8% of them will reach their goals. The rest will wait until next year with an updated wishlist.

Why do so many people lose track of their goals? Most motivators claim the first step is decisive. Nevertheless, people don't have any problems starting — they struggle to finish. For example, some say: “I'll run every morning of this year starting Monday.” Indeed, they will get up, put their trainers on and run. Possibly, most will repeat it on Tuesday; some will continue the exercise on Wednesday. Yet few will work towards that goal regularly.

We respect those who begin but admire the success of those who finish.

The typical way to achieve our desires lies in working hard and struggling until we exhaust ourselves. To many people, that is the price of success. While that may be true for some, there are thousands of disappointed people whose dreams remain unfulfilled. Why? Because when we chase something blindly and push ourselves over our limits, we become critical of ourselves, scrutinizing and diminishing little victories. We end up convincing ourselves that we are not enough, even for ourselves.

The solution is simple: the less we demand perfection, the greater chance we give ourselves to finish what we started. When we don't bother about how great something should be, we work on it without stress and anxiety.

Follow along to learn how to attain your goals without frustration and disappointment, and discover the secret ingredient of success that will prevent procrastination on your way to dreams.

chapters.title: Perfectionism kills your progress
chapters.text:
People plan their goals and build day-by-day schemes to attain them — nevertheless, only some stick to those arrangements. For example, imagine that you decide to take a course. You spend days choosing the best one, then pay for it and mark the first day in the calendar. However, the second day may be busy, and you pass on the studies promising yourself that you'll attend two classes tomorrow. Week after week, you'll keep postponing the promise.

By not keeping your word, you'll stop trusting yourself. Imagine your spouse keeps promising they will go to the theater with you, but they find excuses every time you remind them. Would you still trust them next time? Probably not.

The reason for putting off the planned things lies in the desire to make everything perfect. We don't want to be better dancers or writers — we want to be the best. This overestimation puts us in a position where we dream about the highest level but don't have enough experience and knowledge to get there. Frustrated, we leap from hope to disappointment. Motivational books push us to try harder and persevere. Yet, no one says it's okay to interrupt the process and resume it later.

We may be brave enough to take the first step, but perfectionism prevents us from taking the second, third, and every next step. It's not easy to eradicate this habit. Since childhood, we have strived to be the best at everything we do. Parents push their children to get the highest grades and praise when they are the first in the class. Nevertheless, we put immense pressure on our shoulders by idealizing the top place. We cannot be perfect at everything. And even if we are the most successful in one field, it will not last forever.

Perfectionism forces us to meet unrealistic expectations, thus discouraging us.

Moreover, most believe that everything will go as they planned it. Nonetheless, life is unpredictable, and your agenda will have to adjust accordingly.

So, don't get frustrated the next time you skip a lesson on Tuesday or cannot train for a week. Your path didn't end because you put it on hold. Your journey shouldn't be perfect since the goal is to grow and enjoy the process. Only then will you finish what you’ve started.

chapters.title: The bigger the goal, the greater the pressure
chapters.text:
The disappointment we feel when we abandon a little goal is less brutal than when we turn away from a significant dream. Despite that, motivators persuade us to reach for the moon. Thus, we overestimate our abilities, time, and enthusiasm and end up discouraged.

We'll be too frustrated even to start if the goal is too massive. It can hinder our progress and make us go backward. For example, if you never run but choose to take a marathon, you'll get anxious every time you step on a treadmill. When you start running, you'll see how far you are from your goal, and it's all because you set an unrealistic aim. If you plan to run a few miles, you'll make progress.

The solution is to cut your goal in half. This simple trick will make it more attainable and motivate you because you will feel it closer, notice your progress, and manage it. For instance, decide to lose five pounds if you want to lose ten. You will be happy and satisfied when you lose seven because you have exceeded the expected result by two pounds. Next month, you'll try to lose the other three pounds, and the result will be just the one you wanted.

Goals are a marathon, not a sprint. ~ Jon Acuff

If you cannot lessen the dream, give yourself more time. For example, imagine you have a loan and want to return it in half a year. You will get frustrated if you lose money, or you'll not allow yourself anything because you will be saving. Instead, give yourself a year. You'll have enough to save up and not cry over every little treat you indulge in.

By cutting your goal into chewable pieces, you can progress without procrastination.

Start by analyzing your previous goals and cut each in two. If you want to write 200 pages a day, choose to write 100. You will be happy if you write 150, not angry at yourself. Take little steps toward your purpose. In the long run, they are more effective than giant leaps.

chapters.title: By putting time into one life area, you must take it from another
chapters.text:
When people think about effective time management, they believe it's about getting everything done. Those who balance work and family life know it the best. They use multitasking as their primary tool to harmonize constant chores, child care, work assignments, and other duties while remaining healthy and psychologically sane.

Nevertheless, when setting a goal, you must understand that it will steal time from other spheres of life. There may be less family time around the table or no relaxation with a book. Moreover, consider that by succeeding in one aspect, you'll decline in another. For example, famous scientists, artists, and poets destroyed their relationships because they couldn't contribute enough time and effort.

Perfectionism will push you to struggle and take on more tasks than you can handle. Eventually, you'll burn out and go back to your previous life. Thus, you shouldn't feel ashamed of failing in some fields because it gives you a chance to be better at others.

Remember, no one can be good at everything.

To make more free time for your intention, choose a few things you agree to bomb. It may be cooking dinner on Mondays or watching movies with your spouse. You can always order food or find another hobby for both of you. It shouldn't be permanent. If you have a temporary goal, the change doesn't have to last longer than that. However, you may like the transformation and the sense that you have more free time because you shrug off unnecessary habits.

Sometimes, we have to refuse other people to make space for ourselves. It gets more complicated when you have a family and shared chores. Yet, you can find a solution through open communication with your spouse that will suit you both.

Keep your eyes on the prize; it makes declining irrelevant opportunities easier.

Finally, neglecting things you don't like can be fun. It makes you feel more human and demands less of yourself. So, think of a few things you can be bad at. These can be activities you abandon or relationships you put on hold. If you cannot leave something behind, try to shorten it. Give yourself time and a chance to succeed by making space in your to-do list.

chapters.title: Make your aim attainable. Make it fun
chapters.text:
One of the reasons why people fail is because they push themselves too hard. Instead of motivating, they force themselves to reach their goal. Even if a purpose is noble and desirable, the path to it may be extended and burdensome. For example, imagine that you want to learn a foreign language. It is a serious goal that requires discipline and effort. However, there is more to it than studying books or visiting classes. You watch movies, play games online and even make friends with native speakers.

People believe that you can achieve success only if you struggle and suffer. Some artists and sportsmen stress how tiresome their experience was and how much they had to sacrifice. That may be true, but why don't they highlight how much they enjoyed the ride?

To reach your goal, make it entertaining. If you want to get healthier, dance, walk with a friend, and visit restaurants that offer high-quality food. Hitting the gym or refusing all your favorite dishes are not your only options. If you make your path torturous, you will fail.

The more you adjust your goal to your tastes and preferences, the more chances you'll reach it.

You'll need additional encouragement when your target is not fun. People motivate themselves either by reward or punishment. For instance, deadlines remind people they can fail a project or get fired if they don't do the task on time. Others, on the contrary, think of a promotion they will get if they work harder.

It's vital to know what drives you — reward or punishment. If the former, give yourself presents or do good things for yourself when you make progress. It will boost your reward motivation. If you prefer fear of defeat, remind yourself what you can lose if you fail or what your life will be if you give up. That may be a negative perspective, but it's efficient in short-term tasks.

Hence, make your goal of reaching the experience fascinating. Let it wake you up in the morning, and wait for it throughout the day. Add fun to it in a unique way. For example, if you want to read more books, make it a ritual. Light candles, bring tea, and make yourself cozy so that you'll associate reading with a pleasing atmosphere.

Did you know? According to Dr. Gail Matthews' research at Dominican University in California, writing down your goals increases the likelihood of achieving them by 42%.

chapters.title: Those who never leave their hiding places will never succeed
chapters.text:
Perfectionism can work in mysterious and tricky ways. For example, you may think you are too busy to work, but in reality, you are avoiding the job. People often use activities like watching movies or doing chores as their hiding places. This way, they have an excuse for not moving toward their goal.

Furthermore, many people give up their dreams at the very root when they face noble obstacles like “you don't have enough experience in the field” or “if you start your business, you'll fail your marriage.” Such unreasonable doubts can come from our heads or other people. They often include words like “until,” “if…then,” and “too hard.” For example, you plan to write a novel, but writing a whole book seems too hard, and you won't start until you know how to finish it.

When you get distracted, ask yourself if that is urgent or if you are hiding from the fear of failure.

To overcome these obstacles:

• Make your goals simpler.
• Don't strive to write a whole book; write only a few paragraphs.
• Start small, and you will end big.
• Don't think that you missed the opportunity and that it's too late to succeed. That's an excuse for those who never reach their goals.

People also hinder their progress and sabotage their success because of nasty rules they have lived by for years. Everyone has at least one negative affirmation they adhere to. For example, many believe money is evil or that you must be a genius to become successful. Such beliefs undermine our confidence and force us to give up.

Fortunately, there is a way to break those laws of destruction. Here are the steps you can follow:

• Determine the negative belief. It can be: “If I were rich, I would be a bad person.”
• Ask yourself what that statement means. Why will money make you evil? What terrible things are you going to do with it? Can you use it for good?
• Replace it with a new, positive assertion like “Money is neutral. I can do many great deeds when I am wealthy.”

All in all, be aware of your behavior and thoughts when moving toward your goal. Be prepared to fight perfectionism's obstacles and your mindset to prosper.

chapters.title: When you measure your progress, your confidence rises
chapters.text:
It doesn't take long to despair when you fail. Sometimes the black stripe never seems to end. Nevertheless, you tend to exaggerate your hardships when you lose or suffer from recurrent misfortunes. In reality, though, your progress doesn't deter from a few mistakes. On the contrary, it acquires new qualitative aspects.

Most people are afraid to note down and analyze their progress because they fear finding they are at the same place they were weeks ago. When you put in effort and time but don't move from a starting point, it's high time you check the data to make the necessary adjustments. That's why people write down their measurements before starting a diet and check them weekly. It helps to monitor their weight and motivate them to move forward. If the results aren't pleasant, they can find the reasons and alter their approach.

On the one hand, data is the only source we can trust regarding our progress. People rely on their emotions and opinions, hindering their objective analysis. Unfortunately, we cannot trust ourselves and our memories as much as we can trust the statistics.

So why do people neglect this helpful tool? In most cases, data is a major cause of stress and anxiety. If we don't step on the scales, we won't know how overweight we are. Rough numbers bring pain. They cannot sugarcoat the bad news for you — they make you confront the truth, no matter how brutal it is. However, discomfort means that you are alive, and thus, you can change.

Moving on after a failure builds more substantial barriers against other challenges.

Data helps to see when you must alter the strategy and direction in each area of your life. Hence, you should measure your success regarding business or other goals. For example, you can see how much time you contributed to your plan last week, how many words you wrote, or how many miles ran. You can even track your personality enhancement results by noting the times you got anxious or broke down. No matter what area you are trying to transform, measuring your progress is the only way to keep you just and objective about your effort.

chapters.title: People give up because they fear the outcome
chapters.text:
No marathon runner stops a few steps before the finish line. On the contrary, most athletes run the last few feet on their second breath through the pain and exhaustion because those steps can be decisive in a race. Nonetheless, many people sabotage their victory in the end. A case in point, some artists destroy their work for not being 'good enough,' even when they have practically finished it, and why some writers never get to write “The End” in an epic novel.

Perfectionism reveals itself in our attempts to destroy what we created or leave it undone for the following reasons:

• People are afraid of what comes next. For example, writers anticipate the possible rejections from literary agents or that their creations will stay in the drawers for years.
• Many demand their creation to be perfect, but they feel it isn't. For instance, some people don't watch the series till the end because they fear it will end badly.
• The terror of “what now?” Some individuals leave their goals because they don't know if they'll find a new one. Hence, they keep the unfinished business and those few pounds on their waiting list.

The fear of reaching your goal hinders your progress and makes you stress out.

The other aspect that makes people turn away from their dreams lies in the benefits of not attaining their goals. They believe that if they don't finish, they will:

• Not be saddened by the negative outcome.
• Be praised for their torturing experience and selfless sacrifice to other areas of life like children or a partner.
• Be safe from not matching others' expectations. For example, the artists whose work triumphed fear creating something new because their next paintings may not be such a success.

The harder you try to be perfect, the less likely you'll accomplish your goals. ~ Jon Acuff

Remember that there are no objective reasons for you to leave your dream. Your fears and perfectionism are trying to push you off the track, but it is the last obstacle you must overcome. Victory is just around the corner, so don't be afraid to take those final steps.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
The road to success is full of bumps and holes. If you are not careful enough, your car can break, or you may get a flat tire. Be that as it may, no driver walks home on foot if that happens. They fix the car, exchange the wheel, and avoid the pitfalls. Just like those drivers, people who move to their goals will have to overcome multiple challenges, some of which will come from their minds.

As human beings, we strive to enhance everything and progress in every area of our life. Nonetheless, we can be ruthless to ourselves sometimes. We demand that everything be flawless, so we prefer not to finish at all rather than finish it halfway. Most will judge the shortcuts and choose a long, martyr's way instead.

When you establish a goal, you should never forget a few tips that will keep you safe from the traps of perfectionism:

• Don't give up after the first or the hundredth try.
• Cut your target in two.
• Make time for your goal by neglecting some of your activities: you cannot succeed in everything.
• Make your aim exciting so that you are never bored.
• Limit your distractions and negative beliefs.
• Measure your progress.
• No fear should stop you at the finish line.

With these straightforward rules, you will keep up your goal no matter how hard it is.

Try this

Write down a goal you have struggled with for months or years. Analyze what mistakes you made on the way to it and how perfectionism hindered your progress. Try to reach your goal again, but use the techniques you learned. No matter how often you fail — accept your imperfections and move on.

Read it till the end?
